name: Medb
description: Medb, also known as Maeve, is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, particularly within the Ulster Cycle. She is portrayed as a powerful and ambitious warrior queen of Connacht, known for her fierce independence, strategic acumen, and insatiable desires. Medb's character is complex, embodying both regal authority and unrestrained passion. She is not merely a ruler but a force of nature, driving events through her will and ambition. Her role in the Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley) solidifies her position as a central figure in Irish epic literature. Medb's influence extends beyond the battlefield; she is also associated with sovereignty, fertility, and the land itself, representing the embodiment of female power and agency within the ancient Celtic world. Her relationships, often politically motivated, further contribute to the intricate tapestry of Irish myth, highlighting the dynamic interplay between personal desires and political realities. Medb’s lasting legacy lies in her portrayal as a formidable queen who defied conventional gender roles and exerted significant influence over the fate of her kingdom and beyond.
subtitle: Queen of Connacht

keyMyths.title: The Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley)
keyMyths.description: Medb's most famous exploit is the Táin Bó Cúailnge, where she leads the army of Connacht to steal the Brown Bull of Cooley from Ulster. Driven by a desire to equal her husband Ailill's possessions, Medb's relentless pursuit sets off a chain of events that devastates Ulster, with only the young hero Cú Chulainn standing against her forces. This myth highlights Medb's ambition, strategic prowess, and the destructive consequences of unchecked desire and pride. The raid becomes a bloody and protracted conflict, showcasing the strength and resilience of both sides, and ultimately solidifying Medb's place as a central figure in Irish legend.

keyMyths.title: Medb and Ailill's Wealth
keyMyths.description: The story begins with Medb and her husband Ailill engaging in a detailed inventory of their respective possessions. Finding themselves equal in wealth, save for Ailill's possession of the magnificent bull Finnbennach, Medb becomes determined to acquire a bull of equal or greater value. This quest for equality and superiority drives the plot of the Táin Bó Cúailnge, as Medb's relentless pursuit of the Brown Bull of Cooley plunges the kingdoms into war. This tale exemplifies Medb's strong will and competitive nature.

keyMyths.title: Medb's Many Consorts
keyMyths.description: Medb is known for having numerous consorts, each contributing to her political power and influence. She famously declared that she would only be with a man who was without fear, meanness, or jealousy. Her choice of partners was often strategic, solidifying alliances and expanding her control over territories. This aspect of Medb's character highlights her independence and agency in a patriarchal society, where she used her relationships to further her own ambitions and maintain her position as a dominant ruler. Her relationships underscore her role as a sovereignty figure.
keyMyths.source: Celtic Myths and Legends by Peter Berresford Ellis

sources.author: Peter Berresford Ellis
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Celtic mythology, including detailed accounts of Medb and her role in various myths.
